doc:
/** * Fast method for parsing unsigned integers that are known to fit into * regular 32-bit signed int type. This means that length is * between 1 and 9 digits (inclusive) and there is no sign character. *

* Note: public to let unit tests call it; not meant to be used by any * code outside this package. * * @param ch Buffer that contains integer value to decode * @param off Offset of the first digit character in buffer * @param len Length of the number to decode (in characters) * * @return Decoded {@code int} value */
label: consistent

got.why: switch(len) fall-through cases 9..2 plus the case-1 base term (ch[off+len-1]) decode 1-9 unsigned digits into an int with no sign handling, exactly as documented.

doc:
/**
 * Similar to {@link #parseInt(String)} but for {@code long} values.
 *
 * @param s String that contains {@code long} value to decode
 *
 * @return Decoded {@code long} value
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc claims it decodes a long value like parseInt for longs; code returns a long via parseInt fast-path (len<=9) or Long.parseLong, matching the claim.

doc:
/**
 * Helper method for determining if given String representation of
 * an integral number would fit in 64-bit Java long or not.
 * Note that input String must NOT contain leading minus sign (even
 * if 'negative' is set to true).
 *
 * @param ch Buffer that contains long value to check
 * @param off Offset of the first digit character in buffer
 * @param len Length of the number to decode (in characters)
 * @param negative Whether original number had a minus sign (which is
 *    NOT passed to this method) or not
 *
 * @return {@code True} if specified String representation is within Java
 *   {@code long} range; {@code false} if not.
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method compares digits against MAX_LONG_STR (or MIN_LONG_STR_NO_SIGN when negative) and returns true iff len<cmpLen or equal-length value <= boundary, matching the documented range check with no leading minus sign expected.

doc:
/**
 * Similar to {@link #inLongRange(char[],int,int,boolean)}, but
 * with String argument
 *
 * @param s String that contains {@code long} value to check
 * @param negative Whether original number had a minus sign (which is
 *    NOT passed to this method) or not
 *
 * @return {@code True} if specified String representation is within Java
 *   {@code long} range; {@code false} if not.
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method returns true when unsigned digit string s fits Java long range (using MIN_LONG_STR_NO_SIGN when negative else MAX_LONG_STR); @param s, @param negative, and @return all match code behavior.

doc:
/** * Fast method for parsing integers that are known to fit into * regular 32-bit signed int type. This means that length is * between 1 and 9 digits (inclusive) *

* Note: public to let unit tests call it */
label: inconsistent

got.why: switch handles len 2-9 and the base `num = ch[off+len-1]-'0'` covers len=1, so all 1-9 digit lengths are parsed as claimed; max 9 digits (999999999) fits a signed 32-bit int.

doc:
/**
 * Method to deserialize JSON content into equivalent "tree model",
 * represented by root {@link TreeNode} of resulting model.
 * For JSON Arrays it will an array node (with child nodes),
 * for objects object node (with child nodes), and for other types
 * matching leaf node type
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Code returns `_codec().readTree(this)`, delegating to the codec to build the tree model rooted at a TreeNode exactly as the doc describes; node-type specifics are the codec's contract, not contradicted here.

doc:
/**
 * Method to deserialize JSON content into equivalent "tree model",
 * represented by root {@link TreeNode} of resulting model.
 * For JSON Arrays it will an array node (with child nodes),
 * for objects object node (with child nodes), and for other types
 * matching leaf node type. Empty or whitespace documents are null.
 *
 * @return root of the document, or null if empty or whitespace.
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method delegates to `_codec().readTree(this)`; nothing in this code implements or guarantees the doc's 'Empty or whitespace documents are null' claim (behavior lives in the external codec, not verifiable here).

doc:
/** * Method that fetches next token (as if calling {@link #nextToken}) and * verifies whether it is {@link JsonToken#FIELD_NAME} with specified name * and returns result of that comparison. * It is functionally equivalent to: * 
 *   return (nextToken() == JsonToken.FIELD_NAME) && str.getValue().equals(getCurrentName());
 *
* but may be faster for parser to verify, and can therefore be used if caller * expects to get such a property name from input next. * * @param str Property name to compare next token to (if next token is JsonToken.FIELD_NAME) */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc's stated equivalent `(nextToken() == JsonToken.FIELD_NAME) && str.getValue().equals(getCurrentName())` matches the method body exactly; @param str describes the compared name.

doc:
/** * Method that fetches next token (as if calling {@link #nextToken}) and * if it is {@link JsonToken#VALUE_STRING} returns contained String value; * otherwise returns null. * It is functionally equivalent to: * 
 *   return (nextToken() == JsonToken.VALUE_STRING) ? getText() : null;
 *
* but may be faster for parser to process, and can therefore be used if caller * expects to get a String value next from input. */
label: consistent

got.why: Code `return (nextToken() == JsonToken.VALUE_STRING) ? getText() : null;` matches the doc's stated equivalent and behavior exactly.

doc:
/** * Method that fetches next token (as if calling {@link #nextToken}) and * if it is {@link JsonToken#VALUE_NUMBER_INT} returns 32-bit int value; * otherwise returns specified default value * It is functionally equivalent to: * 
 *   return (nextToken() == JsonToken.VALUE_NUMBER_INT) ? getIntValue() : defaultValue;
 *
* but may be faster for parser to process, and can therefore be used if caller * expects to get a String value next from input. */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc's fetch-token/return-int-on-VALUE_NUMBER_INT-else-default matches code's ternary exactly; the 'faster'/'String value next' prose is intent, not a code-contradicted claim.

doc:
/** * Method that fetches next token (as if calling {@link #nextToken}) and * if it is {@link JsonToken#VALUE_NUMBER_INT} returns 64-bit long value; * otherwise returns specified default value * It is functionally equivalent to: * 
 *   return (nextToken() == JsonToken.VALUE_NUMBER_INT) ? getLongValue() : defaultValue;
 *
* but may be faster for parser to process, and can therefore be used if caller * expects to get a String value next from input. */
label: consistent

got.why: Code returns getLongValue() on VALUE_NUMBER_INT else defaultValue, exactly matching the doc's described behavior and its equivalent snippet.

doc:
/** * Method that can be used to get access to object that is used * to access input being parsed; this is usually either * {@link InputStream} or {@link Reader}, depending on what * parser was constructed with. * Note that returned value may be null in some cases; including * case where parser implementation does not want to exposed raw * source to caller. * In cases where input has been decorated, object returned here * is the decorated version; this allows some level of interaction * between users of parser and decorator object. *

* In general use of this accessor should be considered as * "last effort", i.e. only used if no other mechanism is applicable. */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc says returned value may be null in some cases; code returns null, which the contract explicitly permits (base/no-op implementation).

doc:
/** * Method to call to make this parser use specified schema. Method must * be called before trying to parse any content, right after parser instance * has been created. * Note that not all parsers support schemas; and those that do usually only * accept specific types of schemas: ones defined for data format parser can read. *

* If parser does not support specified schema, {@link UnsupportedOperationException} * is thrown. * * @param schema Schema to use * * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if parser does not support schema */
label: consistent

got.why: Code unconditionally throws UnsupportedOperationException, matching the doc's @throws contract that it is thrown when the parser does not support the schema (this base impl supports none).

doc:

doc:
/**

 * {@link ObjectCodec} is needed for binding data parsed
 * using {@link JsonParser} constructed by this factory
 * (which typically also implies the same for serialization

 *

 */
label: consistent

got.why: Javadoc describes the method's purpose and return semantics; returning false is a valid documented outcome ('false if a general ObjectCodec is enough'), so the code does not contradict the doc.

doc:
/**
 * Method that can be called to push back any content that
 * has been read but not consumed by the parser. This is usually
 * done after reading all content of interest using parser.
 * Content is released by writing it to given stream if possible;
 * if underlying input is byte-based it can released, if not (char-based)
 * it can not.
 *
 * @return -1 if the underlying content source is not byte based
 *    (that is, input can not be sent to {@link OutputStream};
 *    otherwise number of bytes released (0 if there was nothing to release)
 *
 * @throws IOException if write to stream threw exception
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code unconditionally returns -1, matching the doc's stated behavior for non-byte-based sources; no documented claim (0 for nothing released, IOException on write) is contradicted by the stub.

doc:
/**
 * Method that can be called to push back any content that
 * has been read but not consumed by the parser.
 * This is usually
 * done after reading all content of interest using parser.
 * Content is released by writing it to given writer if possible;
 * if underlying input is char-based it can released, if not (byte-based)
 * it can not.
 *
 * @return -1 if the underlying content source is not char-based
 *    (that is, input can not be sent to {@link Writer};
 *    otherwise number of chars released (0 if there was nothing to release)
 *
 * @throws IOException if write using Writer threw exception
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method returns -1, which the doc explicitly documents as a valid return value ('-1 if the underlying content source is not char-based'); a base impl always returning -1 delivers a documented outcome and contradicts no claim.

doc:
/** * Factory method for getting an instance of JSON numeric value * that expresses given unlimited precision floating point value * *

In the event that the factory has been built to normalize decimal * values, the BigDecimal argument will be stripped off its trailing zeroes, * using {@link BigDecimal#stripTrailingZeros()}.

* * @see #JsonNodeFactory(boolean) */

doc:
/**
 * Parse a date from ISO-8601 formatted string. It expects a format
 * [yyyy-MM-dd|yyyyMMdd][T(hh:mm[:ss[.sss]]|hhmm[ss[.sss]])]?[Z|[+-]hh:mm]]
 *
 * @param date ISO string to parse in the appropriate format.
 * @param pos The position to start parsing from, updated to where parsing stopped.
 * @return the parsed date
 * @throws ParseException if the date is not in the appropriate format
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code parses yyyy-MM-dd/yyyyMMdd date, optional T-time (hh:mm[:ss[.sss]]), and required Z or +/- offset, throwing ParseException on malformed input—matching the documented format and @throws claim.

label: consistent

got.why: method returns static TIMEZONE_GMT TimeZone field, matching the doc's 'accessor for static GMT timezone instance'

doc:
/**
 * Format a date into 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ssZ' (GMT timezone, no milliseconds precision)
 *
 * @param date the date to format
 * @return the date formatted as 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ssZ'
 */
label: consistent

got.why: format(date) delegates to format(date, false, TIMEZONE_GMT): false=no milliseconds and TIMEZONE_GMT=GMT, matching the documented 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ssZ' GMT, no-millis output.

doc:

 * Format a date into 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss]Z' (GMT timezone)

 * @return the date formatted as 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss]Z'
 */
label: consistent

got.why: delegates to format(date, millis, TIMEZONE_GMT), matching the doc's GMT timezone, date/millis params, and 'yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss]Z' return format

doc:
/**
 * Format date into yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss][Z|[+-]hh:mm]
 *
 * @param date the date to format
 * @param millis true to include millis precision otherwise false
 * @param tz timezone to use for the formatting (GMT will produce 'Z')
 * @return the date formatted as yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss][Z|[+-]hh:mm]
 */
label: consistent

got.why: code appends '.'+3-digit millis when millis=true, 'Z' when tz.getOffset()==0 else [+-]hh:mm, and base yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss via padInt — exactly matching the documented yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss][Z|[+-]hh:mm] pattern

doc:
/**
 * Parse a date from ISO-8601 formatted string. It expects a format yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss][Z|[+-]hh:mm]
 *
 * @param date ISO string to parse in the appropriate format.
 * @param pos The position to start parsing from, updated to where parsing stopped.
 * @return the parsed date
 * @throws ParseException if the date is not in the appropriate format
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Code parses the documented yyyy-MM-ddThh:mm:ss[.sss][Z|[+-]hh:mm] shape, returns calendar.getTime(), updates pos via pos.setIndex(offset), and throws ParseException on malformed input — all as the doc claims; extra leniency (optional T/seconds/'-') is undocumented behavior, not a contradiction.

doc:

doc:

See {@link #JsonNodeFactory(boolean)} for a full description.

* * @param bigDecimalExact If {code true} DISABLE normalization of {@link BigDecimal} values; * if {code false} ENABLE normalization * @return a factory instance with specified configuration * * @deprecated Use {@link com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.cfg.JsonNodeFeature#STRIP_TRAILING_BIGDECIMAL_ZEROES} * instead for configuring behavior. */

doc:
/**

 *
 * @param startInstant  interval start, in milliseconds
 * @param endInstant  interval end, in milliseconds
 * @param chrono  the chronology to use, not null
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the set exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Code sets all fields from the ms interval via setValues(chrono.get(this, startInstant, endInstant)); startInstant/endInstant are long millis and chrono is used; DateTimeUtils.getChronology(chrono) tolerating null is extra behavior beyond the 'not null' doc, not a contradiction.

doc:
/** * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond duration. *

* When dividing the duration, only precise fields in the period type will be used. * For large durations, all the remaining duration will be stored in the largest * available precise field. * * @param duration the duration, in milliseconds * @param chrono the chronology to use, not null * @throws ArithmeticException if the set exceeds the capacity of the period */
label: inconsistent

got.why: chrono.get(this, duration) divides duration into the period's field types and setValues applies them; throws ArithmeticException per PeriodType capacity, matching the doc's precise-field and overflow claims.

doc:
/** * Append a separator, which is output only if fields are printed before the separator. *

* For example, builder.appendDays().appendSeparator(",").appendHours() * will only output the comma if the days fields is output. *

* The text will be parsed case-insensitively. *

* Note: appending a separator discontinues any further work on the latest * appended field. * * @param text the text to use as a separator * @return this PeriodFormatterBuilder * @throws IllegalStateException if this separator follows a previous one */
label: consistent

got.why: Code calls appendSeparator(text,text,true,false) with useAfter=false, so separator prints only when fields precede it, matching the doc; but the doc omits that parsing is also handled and, more critically, the @throws IllegalStateException claim is delivered by appendSeparator—doc is consistent on behavior. Reconsider: doc makes no false claim, all assertions hold.

doc:
/**
 * Instructs the printer to emit specific text, and the parser to expect it.
 * The parser is case-insensitive.
 *
 * @return this PeriodFormatterBuilder
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if text is null
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code emits/expects literal text and throws IllegalArgumentException when text==null, matching the doc; the case-insensitive parsing claim concerns the parser (Literal), not appendLiteral, and is not contradicted by this method.

doc:
/**
 * Set the minimum digits printed for the next and following appended
 * fields. By default, the minimum digits printed is one. If the field value
 * is zero, it is not printed unless a printZero rule is applied.
 *
 * @return this PeriodFormatterBuilder
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method assigns minDigits to iMinPrintedDigits and returns this, matching the doc's 'set minimum digits' and '@return this PeriodFormatterBuilder'; the default-one and printZero claims are not contradicted by this setter.

doc:
/**
 * Set the maximum digits parsed for the next and following appended
 * fields. By default, the maximum digits parsed is ten.
 *
 * @return this PeriodFormatterBuilder
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method sets iMaxParsedDigits = maxDigits and returns this PeriodFormatterBuilder, matching the doc's description and @return; the 'default is ten' claim concerns a field initializer not shown here and is not contradicted by this snippet.

doc:
/**
 * Reject signed values when parsing the next and following appended fields.
 *
 * @return this PeriodFormatterBuilder
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method sets iRejectSignedValues=v and returns `this` (PeriodFormatterBuilder); doc's 'reject signed values' purpose and '@return this PeriodFormatterBuilder' both hold — undocumented boolean param is under-documentation, not a contradiction.

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond interval.
 *
 * @param startInstant  interval start, in milliseconds
 * @param endInstant  interval end, in milliseconds
 * @param chrono  the chronology to use, null means ISO chronology
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the set exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setValues(chrono.get(this, startInstant, endInstant)) sets all fields from the millis interval using the given chrono (defaulting to ISO via getChronology(null)); the ArithmeticException on overflow arises from the underlying chrono.get/setValues field arithmetic, matching all documented params and behavior.

doc:
/** * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond duration. *

* When dividing the duration, only precise fields in the period type will be used. * For large durations, all the remaining duration will be stored in the largest * available precise field. * * @param duration the duration, in milliseconds * @param chrono the chronology to use, null means ISO chronology * @throws ArithmeticException if the set exceeds the capacity of the period */
label: consistent

got.why: setValues(chrono.get(this, duration)) delegates to the chronology's get(period, duration), which distributes the duration across the period type's precise fields exactly as the doc describes; the doc's ArithmeticException on overflow matches the code's exposed failure path.

doc:
/** * Parses a {@code MutablePeriod} from the specified string. *

* This uses {@link ISOPeriodFormat#standard()}. * * @param str the string to parse, not null * @since 2.0 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code calls parse(str, ISOPeriodFormat.standard()) and returns MutablePeriod, matching the doc's claims about the formatter used, the return type, and the str param.

doc:
/**
 * Adds a duration to this one by dividing the duration into
 * fields and calling {@link #add(ReadablePeriod)}.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration to add, null means add nothing
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code builds `new Period(duration.getMillis(), getPeriodType())` (divides duration into fields) and calls `add(...)` which resolves to `add(ReadablePeriod)` since Period implements ReadablePeriod; the null-guard `if (duration != null)` matches 'null means add nothing'.

doc:
/** * Adds a millisecond duration to this one by dividing the duration into * fields and calling {@link #add(ReadablePeriod)}. *

* When dividing the duration, only precise fields in the period type will be used. * For large durations, all the remaining duration will be stored in the largest * available precise field. * * @param duration the duration, in milliseconds * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period */
label: consistent

got.why: add(long) wraps the millis in new Period(duration, getPeriodType()) and delegates to add(ReadablePeriod); Period's ms-to-fields split only populates precise field types, consistent with the doc's dividing-into-precise-fields claim.

doc:
/** * Adds a millisecond duration to this one by dividing the duration into * fields and calling {@link #add(ReadablePeriod)}. *

* When dividing the duration, only precise fields in the period type will be used. * For large durations, all the remaining duration will be stored in the largest * available precise field. * * @param duration the duration, in milliseconds * @param chrono the chronology to use, null means ISO default * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc claims addition calls add(ReadablePeriod), but code calls add(Period) via add(new Period(duration, getPeriodType(), chrono)); the chrono/PeriodType-based division and precise-field behavior are documented but not visible in this method.

doc:
/** * Merges all the fields from the specified period into this one. *

* Fields that are not present in the specified period are left unaltered. * * @param period the period to set, null ignored * @throws IllegalArgumentException if an unsupported field's value is non-zero */
label: consistent

got.why: mergePeriod delegates to super.mergePeriod(period); the documented merge semantics, null-ignored handling, and IllegalArgumentException on unsupported non-zero fields are the standard contract implemented by the superclass method it calls.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the years field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of years in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getYears() returns getPeriodType().getIndexedField(this, YEAR_INDEX), which returns 0 when the field is unsupported (index -1), matching the doc's 'zero if unsupported'.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the months field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of months in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getIndexedField(this, MONTH_INDEX) returns the months value or 0 when the type index is -1 (unsupported), matching 'number of months, zero if unsupported'

doc:
/**
 * Gets the weeks field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of weeks in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getWeeks() returns getPeriodType().getIndexedField(this, WEEK_INDEX); getIndexedField returns 0 when the field index is unsupported (-1), matching 'zero if unsupported'.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the days field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of days in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getIndexedField delegates to PeriodType.getIndexedField which returns 0 for an unsupported field index (-1), matching 'number of days, zero if unsupported'

doc:
/**
 * Gets the hours field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of hours in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getHours() delegates to getPeriodType().getIndexedField(this, HOUR_INDEX), which returns 0 when the hours field is unsupported (indexed field absent), matching the doc's 'zero if unsupported'.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the minutes field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of minutes in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getMinutes() delegates to getIndexedField, which returns 0 for unsupported fields, matching the doc's 'zero if unsupported' claim; returns the minutes count otherwise

doc:
/**
 * Parses a {@code MutablePeriod} from the specified string using a formatter.
 *
 * @param str  the string to parse, not null
 * @param formatter  the formatter to use, not null
 * @since 2.0
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code calls formatter.parsePeriod(str).toMutablePeriod() returning a MutablePeriod, matching the doc's claim of parsing a MutablePeriod from str via the formatter.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the seconds field part of the period.
 *
 * @return the number of seconds in the period, zero if unsupported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: getSeconds() delegates to getPeriodType().getIndexedField(this, SECOND_INDEX), which returns the seconds count and 0 when the field is unsupported, matching the doc's 'number of seconds, zero if unsupported'.

label: consistent

got.why: clear() calls super.setValues(new int[size()]); a fresh Java int[] is zero-initialized, so all values are reset to zero as documented

doc:
/**
 * Sets the value of one of the fields by index.
 *
 * @param index  the field index
 * @param value  the new value for the field
 * @throws IndexOutOfBoundsException if the index is invalid
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setValue delegates to super.setValue(index, value), which for indexed field access throws IndexOutOfBoundsException on an invalid index; no claim contradicts the code.

doc:
/** * Sets the value of one of the fields. *

* The field type specified must be one of those that is supported by the period. * * @param field a DurationFieldType instance that is supported by this period, not null * @param value the new value for the field * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the field is null or not supported */
label: consistent

got.why: set() delegates to super.setField(field, value); the null/unsupported-field IllegalArgumentException is enforced by the superclass setField, matching the doc's @throws and @param contracts

doc:
/** * Adds to the value of one of the fields. *

* The field type specified must be one of those that is supported by the period. * * @param field a DurationFieldType instance that is supported by this period, not null * @param value the value to add to the field * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the field is null or not supported */
label: consistent

got.why: add() delegates to super.addField(field, value); Javadoc's throws IllegalArgumentException for null/unsupported field matches BasePeriod.addField contract, and no claim contradicts the code.

doc:
/**
 * Adds a period to this one by adding each field in turn.
 *
 * @param period  the period to add, null means add nothing
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the period being added contains a field
 * not supported by this period
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: consistent

got.why: add delegates to super.addPeriod(period), which per Joda-Time adds each field of the period in turn; null adds nothing and field/overflow errors surface as documented IllegalArgumentException/ArithmeticException.

doc:
/**
 * Adds to each field of this period.
 *
 * @param years  amount of years to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param months  amount of months to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param weeks  amount of weeks to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param days  amount of days to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param hours  amount of hours to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param minutes  amount of minutes to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param seconds  amount of seconds to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param millis  amount of milliseconds to add to this period, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the period being added contains a field
 * not supported by this period
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: consistent

got.why: add() uses FieldUtils.safeAdd on each field (throws ArithmeticException on overflow) and setPeriod (which rejects nonzero unsupported fields with IllegalArgumentException); doc's per-field add, zero-if-unsupported, and both exceptions all match.

doc:
/**
 * Adds an interval to this one by dividing the interval into
 * fields and calling {@link #add(ReadablePeriod)}.
 *
 * @param interval  the interval to add, null means add nothing
 * @throws ArithmeticException if the addition exceeds the capacity of the period
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code guards `if (interval != null)` (null adds nothing) and calls `add(interval.toPeriod(getPeriodType()))`, matching the doc's null-handling and delegation to add(ReadablePeriod); the ArithmeticException is an uncontradicted downstream behavioral claim.

doc:
/**
 * Output the time in ISO8601 format (HH:mm:ss.SSSZZ).
 *
 * @return ISO8601 time formatted string.
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: toString delegates to ISODateTimeFormat.time().print(this), which emits the full HH:mm:ss.SSSZZ ISO8601 time the doc promises.

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from another ReadableDuration.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration to set
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if duration is null
 * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if an unsupported field's value is
 * non-zero
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Method delegates to super.setDuration(duration), matching the doc's 'sets all fields from another ReadableDuration'; nothing in the code contradicts the propagated @throws claims.

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go.
 *
 * @param years  amount of years in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param months  amount of months in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param weeks  amount of weeks in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param days  amount of days in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param hours  amount of hours in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param minutes  amount of minutes in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param seconds  amount of seconds in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param millis  amount of milliseconds in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if an unsupported field's value is
 * non-zero
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: setDuration delegates to super.setDuration with all 8 fields; @throws UnsupportedOperationException is a claim about super's behavior, not contradicted by this code

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond duration. If any
 * supported fields are imprecise, an UnsupportedOperationException is
 * thrown. The exception to this is when the specified duration is zero.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration, in milliseconds
 * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if any fields are imprecise
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Method delegates to super.setTotalMillis(duration); signature matches (long duration) and no code here contradicts the documented imprecise-fields exception behavior.

doc:
/**
 * Adds a duration to this one.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration to add
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the duration is null
 * @throws IllegalStateException if the duration is imprecise
 */
label: inconsistent

got.why: add() delegates entirely to super.add(duration); the visible code makes no claim contradicting the documented add-behavior or throws, so nothing is provably inconsistent.

doc:
/**
 * Output the time in ISO8601 format (HH:mm:ss.SSS).
 *
 * @return ISO8601 time formatted string.
 */
label: consistent

got.why: toString() delegates to ISODateTimeFormat.time().print(this), which produces ISO8601 time format HH:mm:ss.SSS as the doc claims; extra offset behavior is not contradicted.

doc: /** * Obtains a {@code LocalTime} set to the current system millisecond time * using ISOChronology in the default time zone. * The resulting object does not use the zone. * * @return the current time, not null * @since 2.0 */
label: consistent

got.why: new LocalTime() constructs current time using ISOChronology in default zone per LocalTime's no-arg contract; doc matches

doc:
/**
 * Gets the number of fields in this partial, which is four.
 * The supported fields are HourOfDay, MinuteOfHour, SecondOfMinute
 * and MillisOfSecond.
 *
 * @return the field count, four
 */
label: consistent

got.why: size() returns 4, matching the doc's claim of four fields

doc:
/** * Gets the value of the field at the specifed index. *

* This method is required to support the ReadablePartial * interface. The supported fields are HourOfDay, MinuteOfHour, * SecondOfMinute and MillisOfSecond. * * @param index the index, zero to three * @return the value * @throws IndexOutOfBoundsException if the index is invalid */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc claims fields HourOfDay/MinuteOfHour/SecondOfMinute/MillisOfSecond, index zero to three, and IndexOutOfBoundsException on invalid index — code's switch cases and default throw match exactly.

doc:
/** * Get the value of one of the fields of time. *

* This method gets the value of the specified field. * For example: * 

 * DateTime dt = new DateTime();
 * int hourOfDay = dt.get(DateTimeFieldType.hourOfDay());
 *
* * @param fieldType a field type, usually obtained from DateTimeFieldType, not null * @return the value of that field * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the field type is null */
label: consistent

got.why: Code throws IllegalArgumentException for null fieldType (matches @throws) and returns fieldType.getField(...).get(...) (matches @return); the extra unsupported-field exception is undocumented behavior, not a contradiction.

doc:
/**
 * Checks if the field type specified is supported by this
 * local time and chronology.
 * This can be used to avoid exceptions in {@link #get(DateTimeFieldType)}.
 *
 * @param type  a field type, usually obtained from DateTimeFieldType
 * @return true if the field type is supported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc claims it checks if a field type is supported and returns true when supported; code returns false for null/unsupported duration/range and true otherwise, matching the claim with no contradiction.

doc:
/**
 * Checks if the duration type specified is supported by this
 * local time and chronology.
 *
 * @param type  a duration type, usually obtained from DurationFieldType
 * @return true if the field type is supported
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc claims true if field type supported; code returns true only when field.isSupported() for time/sub-day types, false for null or unsupported — no over/under-promise.

doc:
/**
 * Gets the chronology of the time.
 *
 * @return the Chronology that the time is using
 */
label: consistent

got.why: method returns iChronology (the Chronology in use); doc's 'the Chronology that the time is using' matches

doc:
/**
 * Compares this ReadablePartial with another returning true if the chronology,
 * field types and values are equal.
 *
 * @param partial  an object to check against
 * @return true if fields and values are equal
 */
label: consistent

got.why: equals compares iChronology (chronology) and iLocalMillis (field values) for LocalTime, and falls back to super.equals which per ReadablePartial contract checks chronology, field types and values — matching the doc's promise

doc:
/** * Compares this partial with another returning an integer * indicating the order. *

* The fields are compared in order, from largest to smallest. * The first field that is non-equal is used to determine the result. *

* The specified object must be a partial instance whose field types * match those of this partial. * * @param partial an object to check against * @return negative if this is less, zero if equal, positive if greater * @throws ClassCastException if the partial is the wrong class * or if it has field types that don't match * @throws NullPointerException if the partial is null */
label: consistent

got.why: compareTo returns 0/-1/1 by field order (via super.compareTo for non-LocalTime), delegating to super which throws ClassCastException on type/field mismatch and NPE on null; the fast LocalTime path preserves identical ordering semantics.

doc:
/** * Returns a copy of this time with the partial set of fields replacing * those from this instance. *

* For example, if the partial contains an hour and minute then those two * fields will be changed in the returned instance. * Unsupported fields are ignored. * If the partial is null, then this is returned. * * @param partial the partial set of fields to apply to this time, null ignored * @return a copy of this time with a different set of fields * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any value is invalid */
label: consistent

got.why: withLocalMillis(getChronology().set(partial, ...)) applies partial fields; null partial returns this; set() ignores unsupported fields and throws IllegalArgumentException on invalid values

doc:
/** * Returns a copy of this time with the specified field set * to a new value. *

* For example, if the field type is hourOfDay then the hour of day * field would be changed in the returned instance. * If the field type is null, then this is returned. *

* These lines are equivalent: * 

 * LocalTime updated = dt.withHourOfDay(6);
 * LocalTime updated = dt.withField(DateTimeFieldType.hourOfDay(), 6);
 *
* * @param fieldType the field type to set, not null * @param value the value to set * @return a copy of this time with the field set * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the value is null or invalid */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc says 'If the field type is null, then this is returned', but code throws IllegalArgumentException("Field must not be null") when fieldType == null.

doc: /** * Obtains a {@code LocalTime} set to the current system millisecond time * using ISOChronology in the specified time zone. * The resulting object does not use the zone. * * @param zone the time zone, not null * @return the current time, not null * @since 2.0 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code returns new LocalTime(zone) using the zone only to read current time and throws NPE on null zone, matching the doc's 'current time in the specified zone', 'not null' param, and 'resulting object does not use the zone' (LocalTime stores no zone).

doc:
/** * Returns a copy of this time with the value of the specified * field increased. *

* If the addition is zero or the field is null, then this * is returned. *

* If the addition causes the maximum value of the field to be exceeded, * then the value will wrap. Thus 23:59 plus two minutes yields 00:01. *

* These lines are equivalent: * 

 * LocalTime added = dt.plusHours(6);
 * LocalTime added = dt.withFieldAdded(DurationFieldType.hours(), 6);
 *
* * @param fieldType the field type to add to, not null * @param amount the amount to add * @return a copy of this time with the field updated * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the value is null or invalid * @throws ArithmeticException if the result exceeds the internal capacity */
label: consistent

got.why: Doc says a null field returns `this`, but code throws IllegalArgumentException("Field must not be null") when fieldType == null.

doc:
/**
 * Obtains a {@code LocalTime} set to the current system millisecond time
 * using the specified chronology.
 * The resulting object does not use the zone.
 *
 * @param chronology  the chronology, not null
 * @return the current time, not null
 * @since 2.0
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Code null-checks chronology (matches '@param chronology not null') and returns new LocalTime(chronology) as the current time (matches '@return the current time'); nothing in the code contradicts the doc.

doc:
/** * Parses a {@code LocalTime} from the specified string. *

* This uses {@link ISODateTimeFormat#localTimeParser()}. * * @param str the string to parse, not null * @since 2.0 */
label: consistent

got.why: parse(str) delegates to parse(str, ISODateTimeFormat.localTimeParser()), matching the doc's claim that it parses a LocalTime using ISODateTimeFormat.localTimeParser()

doc:
/**
 * Parses a {@code LocalTime} from the specified string using a formatter.
 *
 * @param str  the string to parse, not null
 * @param formatter  the formatter to use, not null
 * @since 2.0
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method parses a LocalTime from str via formatter.parseLocalTime(str), matching the doc's claim of parsing a LocalTime from the string using a formatter.

doc:
/** * Constructs a LocalTime from the specified millis of day using the * ISO chronology. *

* The millisOfDay value may exceed the number of millis in one day, * but additional days will be ignored. * This method uses the UTC time zone internally. * * @param millisOfDay the number of milliseconds into a day to convert */
label: consistent

got.why: fromMillisOfDay(millisOfDay) delegates to fromMillisOfDay(millisOfDay, null); passing null chronology yields ISO/UTC default, matching the doc's ISO chronology and overflow-days-ignored claims which the code neither contradicts nor exposes otherwise

doc:
/** * Constructs a LocalTime from the specified millis of day using the * specified chronology. *

* The millisOfDay value may exceed the number of millis in one day, * but additional days will be ignored. * This method uses the UTC time zone internally. * * @param millisOfDay the number of milliseconds into a day to convert * @param chrono the chronology, null means ISO chronology */
label: consistent

got.why: chrono defaults via DateTimeUtils.getChronology(chrono) (null->ISO) and .withUTC() applies UTC internally; the millisOfDay-overflow behavior is delegated to the new LocalTime(millisOfDay, chrono) constructor and not contradicted here.

doc:
/** * Constructs a LocalTime from a java.util.Calendar * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Calendar and assigned to the LocalTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Calendar as a local time, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method ignores the type of the calendar and always * creates a LocalTime with ISO chronology. It is expected that you * will only pass in instances of GregorianCalendar however * this is not validated. * * @param calendar the Calendar to extract fields from * @return the created LocalTime * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */
label: consistent

got.why: Code queries HOUR_OF_DAY/MINUTE/SECOND/MILLISECOND from Calendar and builds a default-ISO LocalTime, throwing IllegalArgumentException on null—matching the doc's field-transfer, ISO-chronology, and null-throw claims.

doc:
/** * Constructs a LocalTime from a java.util.Date * using exactly the same field values. *

* Each field is queried from the Date and assigned to the LocalTime. * This is useful if you have been using the Date as a local time, * ignoring the zone. *

* One advantage of this method is that this method is unaffected if the * version of the time zone data differs between the JDK and Joda-Time. * That is because the local field values are transferred, calculated using * the JDK time zone data and without using the Joda-Time time zone data. *

* This factory method always creates a LocalTime with ISO chronology. * * @param date the Date to extract fields from * @return the created LocalTime * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the calendar is null * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the date is invalid for the ISO chronology */
label: consistent

got.why: Code transfers Date's hour/minute/second/millis fields into a default-ISO LocalTime and throws IllegalArgumentException on null, matching the doc's field-transfer, ISO-chronology, and IllegalArgumentException @throws claims.

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from another ReadableDuration.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration to set, null means zero length duration
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if an unsupported field's value is non-zero
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setDuration delegates to super.setDuration(duration); nothing in the visible code contradicts the doc's claims about null-handling or the IllegalArgumentException, which are delivered by the inherited implementation

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go.
 *
 * @param years  amount of years in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param months  amount of months in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param weeks  amount of weeks in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param days  amount of days in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param hours  amount of hours in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param minutes  amount of minutes in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param seconds  amount of seconds in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @param millis  amount of milliseconds in this duration, which must be zero if unsupported
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if an unsupported field's value is non-zero
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setDuration delegates to super.setDuration with identical params; javadoc's per-field zero-if-unsupported and IllegalArgumentException-on-nonzero contract describes the inherited superclass behavior, which the code neither contradicts nor narrows

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond duration.
 * Only fields that are supported and precise will be set.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration, in milliseconds
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setTotalMillis delegates to super.setTotalMillis(duration), setting fields from the ms duration; the 'only supported and precise fields' behavior is in the inherited superclass implementation, and the doc makes no claim the shown code contradicts

doc:
/**
 * Adds a duration to this one.
 *
 * @param duration  the duration to add, mulls means add nothing
 * @throws IllegalStateException if the duration is imprecise
 */
label: consistent

got.why: add() just delegates to super.add(duration) with no null check or IllegalStateException throw shown; doc promises null-handling and IllegalStateException the visible code does not deliver

doc:
/**
 * Sets all the fields in one go from a millisecond interval.
 *
 * @param startInstant  interval start, in milliseconds
 * @param endInstant  interval end, in milliseconds
 */
label: consistent

got.why: Method signature `setTotalMillis(long startInstant, long endInstant)` matches both documented millisecond params; delegates to super, no claim contradicted.

doc:
/**
 * Sets the number of weeks of the duration.
 *
 * @param weeks  the number of weeks
 * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if field is not supported.
 */
label: consistent

got.why: setWeeks delegates to super.setWeeks(weeks), which per Joda-Time contract throws UnsupportedOperationException when the weeks field is unsupported; doc's @throws and @param both hold.

doc:
/**
 * Adds the specified weeks to the number of weeks in the duration.
 *
 * @param weeks  the number of weeks
 * @throws UnsupportedOperationException if field is not supported.

/** * Generates a crypt(3) compatible hash using the DES algorithm. *

* Using unspecified characters as salt results incompatible hash values. *

* * @param original * plaintext password * @param salt * a two character string drawn from [a-zA-Z0-9./]. The salt may be null, in which case a salt is * generated for you using {@link SecureRandom}. * @return a 13 character string starting with the salt string * @throws IllegalArgumentException * if the salt does not match the allowed pattern */
label: consistent

got.why: Code returns a 13-char buffer starting with salt chars (setCharAt 0/1), throws IllegalArgumentException on CRYPT_SALT_PATTERN mismatch, and generates a salt when null via B64.getRandomSalt(2); no claim is contradicted by the code.

/** * Generates a 32-bit hash from a string with a default seed. * The string is converted to bytes using the default encoding. * This is a helper method that will produce the same result as: * *

 * int hash = MurmurHash2.hash32(bytes, bytes.length, seed);
 *
* * @param text The input string * @return The 32-bit hash * @see #hash32(byte[], int, int) */
label: inconsistent

got.why: Code delegates to hash32(bytes, bytes.length), the default-seed overload, matching the doc's 'default seed' + default-encoding equivalence claim; nothing in the code contradicts it.

doc:

 * int seed = 0xe17a1465;

 * int hash = MurmurHash2.hash64(bytes, bytes.length, seed);
 *
* * @param text The input string * @return The 64-bit hash * @see #hash64(byte[], int, int) */
label: inconsistent

got.why: hash64(String) calls the two-arg hash64(bytes, bytes.length) which applies default seed 0xe17a1465, and GET_BYTES_CHARSET is UTF-8, matching the doc's stated equivalence.

doc:

The salt may be null, in which case a salt is * generated for you using {@link B64#getRandomSalt(int)}. * @return a 13 character string starting with the salt string * @throws IllegalArgumentException * if the salt does not match the allowed pattern */
label: consistent

got.why: null salt calls B64.getRandomSalt(2), invalid salt throws IllegalArgumentException via CRYPT_SALT_PATTERN check, buffer fills indices 0-12 (13 chars) with salt at positions 0-1, matching all doc claims

/**
 * Encodes binary data using the base64 algorithm but does not chunk the output.
 *
 * NOTE:  We changed the behaviour of this method from multi-line chunking (commons-codec-1.4) to
 * single-line non-chunking (commons-codec-1.5).
 *
